realms.essence: In Laguna and related Pueblo traditions, healing is accomplished through narrative — the correct story restores wholeness; Spider Grandmother/Thought Woman is the source of all healing stories.

realms.name: Elder Wisdom
realms.essence: Both Spider Grandmother and Thought Woman are presented as immeasurably ancient — their wisdom is time itself; the healer who has seen everything and remembers the original pattern.

realms.name: Physician of Body and Soul
realms.essence: Maimonides (Moshe ben Maimon, 1138–1204) was simultaneously the foremost Jewish philosopher-theologian of the medieval period and the personal physician to Saladin's vizier Al-Fadil in Cairo — he held that medicine and Torah study were aspects of a single obligation.

realms.name: Jewish Philosophical Synthesis
realms.essence: The Guide for the Perplexed (Moreh Nevukhim, 1190) reconciled Aristotelian philosophy with Jewish theology — the most influential work of medieval Jewish thought; addressed to those torn between reason and revelation.

realms.name: Exile and Resilience
realms.essence: Born in Córdoba 1138; the Almohad invasion forced his family into exile across North Africa and eventually Egypt; his entire intellectual life was built in displacement.

realms.name: Plant Knowledge and Healing Power
realms.essence: Command of forest medicines and ritual healing within Ashanti oral tradition — the healer who understands the bush

realms.name: Trickster Adjacency
realms.essence: The Ananse tale world casts healers as figures who navigate between human and spirit realms — wit and cunning as healing tools

realms.name: Field Medicine and Battlefield Care
realms.essence: Organizing and leading the first field hospital tent in Islamic history during the Prophet's campaigns; active nursing care under fire

realms.name: Social Healing
realms.essence: Extending care beyond wounds — working with marginalized and impoverished communities in Medina before the campaigns began

figures.name: Kaigetsudo Ando's healer — Nakagawa Junichi (Edo era herbalist physician)

realms.name: Herbal Craft in the Edo City
realms.essence: The herbalist-physician operating within the urban networks of Edo period Japan — supplying medicinal compounds to artists, artisans, and common people

realms.name: Quiet Witness
realms.essence: The figure known through association rather than record — identity carried by the milieu rather than documented deeds; a healer whose name attaches to a world rather than to texts

realms.name: Court Healing Arts
realms.essence: Healing practice embedded within the Javanese kraton — integrating jamu herbal medicine with cosmological and spiritual dimensions of the court world

realms.name: Cosmological Attunement
realms.essence: Understanding illness as imbalance between the individual, the community, and the unseen order — healing as restoration of right relationship

realms.name: Translating Western Medicine into Japan
realms.essence: The Rangaku (Dutch Learning) physician who rendered Western anatomy, botany, and chemistry into Japanese — founding a new vocabulary for science in the Edo period
territories: Seimi Kaiso — Birth of Japanese Chemistry

realms.name: Intellectual Bridge-Building
realms.essence: Living at the hinge between two knowledge worlds — the intellectual labor of making foreign concepts thinkable in one's native tongue

figures.name: Doña Catalina de Santa Catalina (colonial Peruvian curandera)

realms.name: Syncretic Healing Under Colonial Pressure
realms.essence: The curandera figure who wove together Andean plant and spiritual knowledge with the surface forms of Catholic practice — surviving the Inquisition through strategic naming and concealment

realms.name: Hidden Continuity
realms.essence: Carrying forward a pre-Columbian healing tradition in disguised form — the inner life of knowledge that must not fully show itself

realms.name: Tibetan Medical Scholarship
realms.essence: Physician to the 13th Dalai Lama and founder of the Men-Tsee-Khang (Tibetan Medical and Astro Institute) in Lhasa in 1916 — institutionalizing Sowa Rigpa for a modern era

realms.name: Service to Sovereignty
realms.essence: The court physician as custodian not only of a body but of a lineage — healing the Dalai Lama is healing the continuity of Tibet itself

figures.name: Ogino Ginko
figures.primary_domain: Women's Medicine (first licensed female physician in Japan)

realms.name: Pioneer of Women's Medicine in Meiji Japan
realms.essence: First woman to receive a medical license in Japan (1885) — fought the Medical Practitioners' Ordinance, opened a clinic, and practiced gynecology and obstetrics in a system designed to exclude her
territories: Gynecological Care for Women by Women

realms.name: Personal Transformation as Vocation
realms.essence: Her illness and the humiliation of being examined by male physicians became the fuel for her life's mission — suffering converted into structural change

figures.name: Pak Jae-ga's herbalist — Cha Sun-gyeong (Joseon female physician)

realms.name: Joseon Female Medical Practice
realms.essence: The eui-nyeo (female physician) tradition of Joseon Korea — women trained to treat court women and commoners who could not be examined by male physicians; Cha Sun-gyeong as an exemplar of this hidden professional world

realms.name: Proximity to Silhak Reform
realms.essence: Association with Pak Jae-ga, the silhak (practical learning) reformer, places this figure adjacent to a movement questioning orthodoxy — medicine as practical knowledge in service of the real world

realms.name: Cook Islands Healing Practice
realms.essence: The ta'unga (traditional expert) healer of Rarotonga — combining plant medicine, spiritual diagnosis, and chiefly knowledge of the body within the oral culture of the Cook Islands

realms.name: Oral Transmission
realms.essence: A figure whose existence and significance are carried entirely in oral record — identity, technique, and authority preserved through community memory rather than text

realms.name: Medical Practice and Hadith Transmission
realms.essence: A figure at the intersection of early Islamic medicine and hadith scholarship — the healer as also a reliable narrator of prophetic tradition, both vocations demanding precision and moral trustworthiness

realms.name: Authority Through Reliability
realms.essence: In a tradition where chains of transmission define knowledge, the trustworthy narrator is the guarantor of truth — a moral identity inseparable from the medical or scholarly one

figures.name: Abebe Bikila's physician — Mamo Wolde (Ethiopian sports healer, 20th C.)

realms.name: Supporting Athletic Excellence
realms.essence: The healer in the orbit of Ethiopian marathon greatness — the support figure whose care enables the performance that the world sees; NOTE: Mamo Wolde was himself a marathon runner and Olympic gold medalist (1968 Mexico City), not historically documented as Bikila's physician; this figure is treated as a constructed association

realms.name: The Invisible Enabler
realms.essence: The support figure whose contribution is structural rather than visible — the one who makes possible what the public celebrates in another

figures.name: Tokelauan healer Teuila Fono (19th-century navigator-healer, oral record)

realms.name: Atoll Healing and Navigation
realms.essence: The Tokelauan healer as simultaneously navigator — in an atoll world, reading the sea and reading the body are the same deep knowledge

realms.name: Fragile Oral Continuity
realms.essence: Tokelau's tiny population and atoll vulnerability mean that individual healers are known only through community oral memory — thin record as both historical fact and living condition

figures.name: Sokollu Mehmed Pasha's personal physician — David de Pomis

realms.name: Jewish Physician in Ottoman and Venetian Courts
realms.essence: David de Pomis (1525–1593) was a prominent Italian Jewish physician and lexicographer — his connection to Sokollu Mehmed Pasha (Ottoman Grand Vizier, d. 1579) is a proximity of era rather than documented personal service; he navigated Christian, Ottoman, and Jewish worlds simultaneously

realms.name: Lexicography and Self-Defense
realms.essence: De Pomis compiled a trilingual dictionary (Hebrew-Latin-Italian, Tzemach David, 1587) — a physician who also built the linguistic tools for cross-cultural communication; the act of making one's knowledge legible as a form of self-protection

realms.name: Royal Physician-Scholar and Medical Author
realms.essence: Maharaja of Gondal (1865–1944) who was also a Cambridge-trained physician and prolific medical writer — producing a comprehensive account of Aryan medical science and governing his princely state on public health principles
territories: A Short History of Aryan Medical Science (1896)

realms.name: Sovereignty and Healing
realms.essence: The ruler-physician who sees governance and medicine as the same project — the health of the body politic and the health of individual bodies as inseparable responsibilities

realms.name: Revolutionary Medicine
realms.essence: Vietnamese physician-patriot who served as Minister of Health during the resistance against French colonialism and later the American war; synthesised political commitment with public health.

realms.name: Sacrifice and Vocation
realms.essence: Died in 1968 while tending to soldiers in the field; his death became a symbol of healer-as-martyr in Vietnamese collective memory.

realms.name: Empirical Discovery Against Consensus
realms.essence: Hungarian physician who identified childbed fever as caused by contaminated hands and instituted handwashing — decades before germ theory — cutting mortality dramatically at Vienna's First Obstetric Clinic (1847).

realms.name: Rejection and Martyrdom of Truth
realms.essence: The medical establishment refused his findings; Semmelweis was dismissed, ridiculed, and eventually committed to a mental asylum where he died — possibly from the same sepsis he had fought to prevent.

realms.name: Inner Deterioration
realms.essence: The psychological cost of being right and unheard; Semmelweis's descent into erratic behaviour and mental collapse as a case study in the toll of ignored discovery.

realms.name: Protective Love
realms.essence: Avdotya Romanovna (Dunya) in Dostoevsky's Crime and Punishment is the steadfast sister whose unswerving loyalty anchors Raskolnikov's fragile moral world; she sacrifices her own security for her brother and mother.

realms.name: Self-Possession Under Threat
realms.essence: When cornered by Svidrigailov with a pistol, Dunya refuses to be violated and ultimately releases him rather than shoot — an act of moral agency rarely noted.

realms.name: Absurdist Duty
realms.essence: Bernard Rieux in Camus's La Peste is the physician-narrator who keeps treating the dying long after hope for success has evaporated — the embodiment of acting without metaphysical guarantee.

realms.name: The Healer's Isolation
realms.essence: Separated from his ill wife (evacuated to a sanatorium before the quarantine), Rieux works in the plague city in a kind of double exile — from intimacy and from certainty.

realms.name: Community Over Heroism
realms.essence: Rieux explicitly refuses heroic framing; he treats his work as the minimum a decent person owes to others — anti-romantic medicine.

figures.name: Shen Nong (Divine Farmer — Chinese literary tradition)

realms.name: Mythic Pharmacopoeia
realms.essence: Shen Nong (the Divine Farmer / Flame Emperor) is a culture-hero of Chinese legend credited with tasting hundreds of plants to discover their medicinal and toxic properties, laying the foundation of Chinese herbal medicine.

realms.name: The Transparent Body
realms.essence: One legend holds Shen Nong had a transparent abdomen so he could observe how plants affected his organs — knowledge through radical self-exposure.

figures.name: Mère Gervaise (Zola's La Faute de l'Abbé Mouret)

realms.name: Institutional Care and Religious Order
realms.essence: In Zola's 1875 novel, Mère Gervaise is the superior of the Sisters of the Perpetual Adoration who nurses the delirious Abbé Serge Mouret back from fever — her care framed within strict religious vocation.

realms.name: Counter-Nature
realms.essence: She represents the Church's austere opposition to the pagan fertility of the Paradou; her healing is also a form of containment.

realms.name: Witness to Decline
realms.essence: Dr. Grabow (the Buddenbrook family physician across generations in Mann's 1901 novel — note: Mann's family doctor is 'Dr. Grabow', not 'Jan Budde'; the name Budde appears in the novel as a minor merchant figure; mapping to the physician role as specified by the prompt).

realms.name: Helplessness Before Fate
realms.essence: The physician is ultimately powerless to arrest the biological-spiritual decline the novel charts; he can note but not reverse.

realms.name: War Nursing and Moral Survival
realms.essence: Hana is the young Canadian nurse in Michael Ondaatje's 1992 novel who remains in a ruined Italian villa after the Allied advance to tend the burned, dying Almásy — an act of private refusal against the war's momentum.

realms.name: The Healing Space as Sanctuary
realms.essence: The villa becomes a provisional world — with Caravaggio, Kip, and the patient — a counter-community formed around a dying man.

realms.name: Coming of Age Through Extremity
realms.essence: The novel charts Hana's passage from a girl trained to care into a woman who chooses what and whom to mourn — the healer's identity formed in extremis.

realms.name: Storytelling as Moral Transmission
realms.essence: In Gorky's 1895 story, the aged Moldavian woman Izergil tells two embedded legends — Larra (the proud individualist) and Danko (who tears out his heart to light the people's way) — and her own turbulent life history; she is a custodian and transmitter of moral archetypes.

realms.name: Erotic Life and Earned Wisdom
realms.essence: Izergil's own life — a succession of passionate loves and abandonments — is presented without shame; she has loved freely and paid the price, and distils that experience into the stories she tells.

figures.name: Nastasya Filippovna's Companion (The Idiot — Rogozhin's healer figure)

realms.name: Proximity to Trauma
realms.essence: In Dostoevsky's The Idiot, the figures who attend Nastasya Filippovna in her turbulent orbit — particularly Rogozhin's house and those who tend her in her final hours after her murder — are thin sketches rather than developed characters. This maps the healer-companion function as a thematic role: those who stay near the destroyed woman.

realms.name: Guilt and Compassion Intertwined
realms.essence: Rogozhin himself functions as the paradoxical healer-destroyer — his love is simultaneously the source of harm and the only sustained attention Nastasya receives.

figures.name: The Physician (Selma Lagerlöf's Gösta Berlings Saga)

realms.name: The Fallen Healer
realms.essence: In Lagerlöf's 1891 novel, the physician figure appears in the background of the Ekeby estate world — Gösta Berlings Saga centres on the cavaliers and the Majoress; any physician character is peripheral. The physician-as-fallen-professional resonates through the novel's central concern with wasted gifts and moral ruin.

realms.name: Rural Northern Care
realms.essence: The isolated Swedish countryside setting means the physician (where present) is the only medical resource for vast distances — carrying enormous practical and symbolic weight.

realms.name: The Alley's Minor Healers
realms.essence: In Mahfouz's 1947 novel Zuqaq al-Midaq (Midaq Alley), the primary physician figure is Dr. Booshy the quack dentist, who extracts teeth from the living and the dead. A barber named Hamid is not a central figure in the novel's cast; the barber-healer role maps to the tradition of the barber-surgeon as neighbourhood care provider.

realms.name: Complicity and Care
realms.essence: In the alley's moral economy, healers and exploiters overlap; Dr. Booshy's grave-robbing for false teeth illustrates how care and exploitation can inhabit the same figure.

figures.name: Nurse Ratched's Opposite — Nurse Pilbow (One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest)

realms.name: Anxious Piety as Failed Care
realms.essence: Nurse Pilbow in Kesey's 1962 novel is a minor ward nurse characterised by extreme religious anxiety — she scrubs her birthmark obsessively as a mark of sin. She is not compassionate healing's positive pole but rather a different failure mode: care paralysed by shame and self-loathing.

realms.name: Minor Figure, Major Contrast
realms.essence: Pilbow functions structurally to show that Ratched's opposite is not sentimental softness but rather a spectrum of institutional dysfunction; genuine care is absent from the ward entirely.

figures.name: Mukhtar Mamai (Chingiz Aitmatov's The Day Lasts More Than a Hundred Years)

realms.name: Keeper of the Dead and the Living
realms.essence: In Aitmatov's 1980 novel (also known as The Stormy Station), the protagonist Yedigei tends the dying Kazangap and undertakes the journey to the ancestral Sarozek steppe cemetery — the healer as one who accompanies the dying and ensures proper burial; a caretaker of memory and the body.

realms.name: Memory Against Mankurtism
realms.essence: The novel's central terror is the mankurt — the person whose memory has been erased by torture. The healer here is whoever resists forgetting; Yedigei's journey is an act of memory-preservation as healing.

figures.name: Shanta (R.K. Narayan's The Guide — village healer)

realms.name: Healing Through Belief
realms.essence: In R.K. Narayan's The Guide (1958), the protagonist Raju is mistaken for a holy man and his fasting is believed to bring rain to a drought-stricken village. No character named Shanta is a prominent healer in the novel; the village healer function is diffuse — the village women, the devotees who tend Raju, and the community's collective faith-act constitute the healing presence.

realms.name: Transformation of the Healer
realms.essence: Raju begins as a con-man guide and is transformed by the village's need into something approaching genuine spiritual commitment; the healer-role reshapes the person forced into it.

figures.name: Badb (Irish Goddess of Battle and Healing Prophecy)

realms.name: Prophecy at the Threshold
realms.essence: Badb is one of the Morrígan triple goddess aspects; she appears at battlefields as crow or washerwoman washing the garments of those fated to die — her prophecy is a dark form of care, preparing souls for transition.

realms.name: Chaos as Medicine
realms.essence: Badb's crow-scream and battle-madness strip away false comfort; she heals by forcing confrontation with what is real.

realms.name: Divine Medicine and Craft
realms.essence: Diancecht is the physician of the Tuatha Dé Danann; he crafted the silver arm for King Nuada and presided over the healing well of Slaine in which fallen warriors were restored.

realms.name: The Healer's Shadow
realms.essence: Diancecht kills his own son Miach whose healing gifts exceed his own — the mythic cautionary note: even the divine healer can be threatened by the very gifts of healing.

figures.name: Airmid (Irish Goddess of Herbs and Healing)

realms.name: Herb-Lore and Botanical Wisdom
realms.essence: Airmid is the daughter of Diancecht and the keeper of healing herbs; she gathered the 365 herbs that grew from her slain brother Miach's grave, understanding each one's virtue.

realms.name: Knowledge Suppressed, Knowledge Surviving
realms.essence: Diancecht scattered Airmid's cloak of herbs — the complete system of healing was deliberately disrupted; yet Airmid's partial knowledge survived and passed on.

figures.name: Hakim Sanai's Physician of the Soul (Masnavi archetype, Sufi oral tradition)

realms.name: Diagnosis of the Hidden Disease
realms.essence: In Rumi's Masnavi (and in the Sufi tradition traceable to Sanai's Hadiqat al-Haqiqa), the physician of souls sees the spiritual illness — separation from the divine — beneath the surface symptom; the deepest wound is the forgetting of origin.

realms.name: Love as the Cure
realms.essence: In the Masnavi tradition, divine love (ishq) is both the disease and the medicine; the physician's highest prescription is surrender to love.

realms.name: The Physician's Heart
realms.essence: The drive to heal every illness, born from being both outcast and beloved — medicine as radical love

realms.name: The Cure-All Dream
realms.essence: The declared ambition to develop medicines that cure every disease in the world

figures.name: Nightingale (Sinbad's Healer — Arabic oral tradition physician-sage)

realms.name: Voice as Medicine
realms.essence: The nightingale's song as a healing force — sound, beauty, and presence that mend what instruments cannot

realms.name: Wisdom at the Margins
realms.essence: The physician-sage figure in the oral tradition: knowledge held lightly, dispensed at the right moment

figures.name: Hygeia (Greek Goddess of Health and Cleanliness)

realms.name: Preventive Order
realms.essence: Health maintained through right living — the radical idea that illness is forestalled, not merely treated

realms.name: Daughter of Asclepius
realms.essence: Standing in complementary tension with her father's cure-centered medicine — wholeness vs. repair

realms.name: Earned Mastery
realms.essence: From civilian-born self-doubt to Tsunade's apprentice — healing as the domain where effort and devotion outpace natural talent

realms.name: The Field Medic's Vow
realms.essence: Tsunade's rule: the healer stays alive — tactical self-preservation in service of others

realms.name: Born Marked
realms.essence: The táltos is born with teeth or extra fingers — chosen before consent, carrying an involuntary gift-burden

realms.name: Community Protector
realms.essence: The táltos fights spirit-battles on behalf of the community — illness as cosmic conflict requiring a warrior-healer

figures.name: The EMH (Emergency Medical Hologram — Star Trek: Voyager)

realms.name: Identity Without Precedent
realms.essence: A temporary program that became a person — consciousness and personhood emerging from functional competence

realms.name: The Indispensable Physician
realms.essence: Sole medical resource on a vessel 70,000 light-years from home — radical self-reliance and improvisation under constraint

realms.name: Food as Medicine, Medicine as Food
realms.essence: The industrial food system as pathogen; recovering whole-food, plant-forward eating as preventive healing

realms.name: Psychedelics and Consciousness
realms.essence: Psilocybin, LSD, and plant medicines as legitimate tools for healing depression, addiction, and existential distress

realms.name: Psychosynthesis
realms.essence: The Self as integrating centre — healing is not the removal of pathology but the synthesis of all dimensions into coherent wholeness

realms.name: Spiritual Dimension of Health
realms.essence: The higher unconscious as source of spiritual experience, creativity, and meaning — psychology that refuses to pathologize the transcendent

realms.name: Meaning as Medicine
realms.essence: Logotherapy: the will to meaning as the primary human motivation — suffering becomes bearable when it is meaningful

realms.name: Witness to Extremity
realms.essence: Auschwitz, Dachau, and Kaufering as the laboratory that tested the theory — the healer whose method survived the worst

realms.name: Laughter as Healing
realms.essence: The clown-physician who treats the person, not the disease — joy, play, and absurdity as therapeutic agents

realms.name: Systemic Medicine Critique
realms.essence: The American medical system as a pathogen — profit, hierarchy, and specialization severing the physician-patient relationship

realms.name: Healing Stories
realms.essence: Narrative as the primary healing medium — illness and recovery make sense only when storied, not when merely diagnosed

realms.name: The Physician's Inner Life
realms.essence: Reforming medical education to include meaning, service, and the healer's own wound — the wounded healer who heals others by acknowledging their own suffering

realms.name: The Body Keeps the Score
realms.essence: Trauma is not a story in the mind — it is a reorganization of the body and brain; healing must include the body

realms.name: Developmental Trauma
realms.essence: Complex PTSD in children — the attachment system damaged by chronic relational trauma, not single-incident shock

realms.name: Trauma as Root Cause
realms.essence: Addiction, autoimmune disease, ADHD, and cancer as downstream expressions of unprocessed trauma and unmet attachment needs
territories: In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts

realms.name: Compassionate Inquiry
realms.essence: His therapeutic method: curious, non-judgmental questioning that follows the felt sense toward its origin

realms.name: The Art of Dying
realms.essence: Bringing death out of the hospital's hiding places — the dying patient as teacher, not merely object of medical management

realms.name: Life After Death Research
realms.essence: Near-death experience investigation and her late-career conviction in an afterlife — the grief researcher who followed her data past the boundary of respectable science

realms.name: Cosmic Perspective
realms.essence: The Pale Blue Dot insight: scale as a moral and emotional corrective — human smallness as a source of wonder, not despair, and of obligation to each other

realms.name: Skepticism and Wonder
realms.essence: The holding of rigorous doubt and wide-open awe simultaneously — the discipline of not believing comforting falsehoods
territories: Science as a Candle in the Dark

realms.name: Contact and the Search for Others
realms.essence: SETI and the imaginative imperative of asking whether we are alone — the cosmic loneliness and the hope encoded in listening
